quick_facts.label: Qualification level
quick_facts.value: A postgraduate master's pathway in interior design. Under the UGC National Credit Framework, a two-year master's programme entered after a three-year bachelor's degree is positioned as a master's qualification and requires at least 80 credits across the two years; individual universities determine the programme title and curriculum.

quick_facts.label: Typical duration
quick_facts.value: Two years is the usual full-time duration. JECRC University’s 2026–27 fee schedule lists M.Sc. Interior Design with a duration of two years, while SNDT Women’s University describes its M.Sc. Interior Design as a two-year, four-semester, full-time on-campus programme.

quick_facts.label: Entry requirement
quick_facts.value: A B.Sc. in Interior Design is a directly relevant preparation for this node, but eligibility is institution-specific. For example, JECRC University’s current M.Sc. Interior Design page accepts a graduation-level degree or diploma in any discipline from a recognised institution with at least 60% aggregate, subject to its listed category relaxation.

quick_facts.label: Published fee example
quick_facts.value: For 2026–27, JECRC University lists ₹1,40,000 as the annual fee for M.Sc. Interior Design, comprising ₹98,000 tuition and ₹42,000 other fee, plus a one-time non-refundable ₹10,000 admission and alumni fee. This is one university’s published schedule, not an India-wide fee benchmark; fees, inclusions, scholarships and revisions vary by institution and year.

sections.heading: Eligibility after a B.Sc. in Interior Design
paragraphs: A B.Sc. in Interior Design is an appropriate academic progression for an M.Sc. in Interior Design, but there is no single India-wide admission rule for this named programme. Applicants should compare the target university’s accepted qualifying degrees, minimum aggregate, category-based relaxation, portfolio requirements and any test or interview requirements. As a current example, JECRC University states that applicants need a recognised graduation-level degree or diploma in any discipline with at least 60% aggregate, with a stated 5% relaxation for specified categories.
bullets: Keep final-year marksheets, degree/provisional certificate and category documents ready where applicable.
Where the university specifies a design-related undergraduate subject, confirm that the B.Sc. in Interior Design transcript meets that wording before applying.
Do not assume that eligibility at one M.Sc. Interior Design provider applies at another.

sections.heading: Duration and study pattern
paragraphs: The conventional format is a two-year postgraduate programme. The UGC National Credit Framework describes a two-year master's programme following a three-year bachelor's degree as requiring a minimum of 80 credits, with at least 40 credits in each year. Programme delivery can differ: SNDT Women’s University labels its M.Sc. Interior Design as two years and four semesters, while JECRC’s 2026–27 schedule also lists two years for M.Sc. Interior Design.
bullets: Expect the institution to specify its own semester, credit, attendance, studio and assessment rules.
Confirm whether the programme is full-time and campus-based, as mode of delivery is not uniform across providers.

sections.heading: What the curriculum can cover
paragraphs: Course content is university-specific rather than nationally standardised for this degree title. A Thiruvalluvar University CBCS/LOCF curriculum for M.Sc. Interior Design and Decor illustrates the advanced mix that may appear: history of design, application of art and design, practical space planning, furniture design, landscaping and ornamental gardening, research methodology and statistics, textile science, practical furnishing in interiors, visual merchandising, renewable-energy-related study and styles in interiors.

The programme should therefore be evaluated through its actual syllabus—not its title alone. For a prospective student, the most useful comparison points are the balance of studio/practical work and theory, software and visualisation training, materials and services, sustainability, research methods, electives, fieldwork, and the final project or dissertation requirements where offered.
bullets: Request the current semester-wise syllabus and scheme of examination before accepting an offer.
Check whether electives match the intended focus, such as furniture, lighting, sustainable interiors, visual merchandising or landscape-related work.
Review the stated practical/studio components, rather than assuming every M.Sc. has the same technical or design-software coverage.

sections.heading: Admission route
paragraphs: Admission route and selection method vary by university. JECRC University provides an online application route for its M.Sc. Interior Design and says an admissions representative will assist after submission. SNDT Women’s University lists on-campus admission through CET, a subject-specialisation test and interview for its named M.Sc. Interior Design programme. Applicants should use the target institution’s current admission notice rather than relying on a generic national entrance-exam assumption.
bullets: Apply through the university’s official admissions portal and retain acknowledgement and payment records.
Check the live programme page for the current intake, application-round status, documents, test/interview requirements and fee deadlines; these change by admission cycle.
If applying from a B.Sc. in Interior Design programme, submit a transcript that makes the relevant design coursework clear.

sections.heading: Fees and budgeting
paragraphs: Costs must be treated as dated, provider-specific information. In its 2026–27 schedule, JECRC University publishes ₹1,40,000 annual fee for M.Sc. Interior Design—₹98,000 tuition plus ₹42,000 other fee—with a separate one-time ₹10,000 admission and alumni fee. Its schedule shows ₹80,000 as the first instalment plus admission and alumni fee, followed by a ₹70,000 subsequent instalment. Housing, transport, software, printing, model-making and materials may be separate, so applicants should obtain the full written cost sheet from each provider.
bullets: Distinguish recurring annual academic fees from one-time admission charges.
Ask whether studio consumables, software licences, site visits, examination fees, accommodation and transport are included.
Check scholarship conditions carefully: eligibility, renewal rules and whether a scholarship can be combined with external financial assistance can differ by university.

sections.heading: Options after the degree
paragraphs: A research-oriented next step can be a Ph.D. in an appropriate design, interior-design, built-environment or allied discipline, subject to the admitting university’s discipline-matching and entrance rules. UGC regulations state that master's degree holders with at least 55% aggregate or an equivalent grade are eligible to seek Ph.D. admission, with a 5% relaxation for specified categories; the receiving institution’s current Ph.D. rules still govern the actual application and selection process.
bullets: For professional practice, strengthen a portfolio with completed spatial-design projects, technical drawings, specifications, visualisations and site or execution experience.
For research or teaching-oriented goals, select an M.Sc. curriculum with research methods and substantial supervised project work, then verify Ph.D. eligibility at the intended host university.

Related postgraduate alternatives may use different degree titles, including M.Des. or M.Arch-related interior/space-design programmes; compare curriculum and entry rules rather than treating them as interchangeable.
